title: SuperBEST v3 Cost Analysis — Biology Block 5: Spectroscopy, Membrane Potential, Pharmacokinetics

division_decomposition.note: SuperBEST has no 'div' primitive. Division a/b = mul(a, recip(b)).
division_decomposition.cost: recip(b)=2 + mul(a, recip(b))=2 → total 4 nodes per division

log_base_10_decomposition.note: SuperBEST has no log10 primitive. log10(x) = ln(x)/ln(10) = mul(ln(x), recip(ln(10))).
log_base_10_decomposition.cost: ln(x)=1 + ln(10)=1 (constant terminal) + recip(ln10)=2 + mul(ln_x, recip_ln10)=2 → 6 nodes

equations.name: Beer-Lambert law — Absorbance form
equations.type: pure product — no exp, no ln
equations.notes: Trivial triple product. Three mul-chained gives 2+2=4 nodes. No division, no transcendental functions.

equations.name: Beer-Lambert law — Transmittance form
equations.notes: The exponent −εcl is built with two mul nodes and one neg. The outer exp costs just 1 node. Total 7 nodes.

equations.name: Beer-Lambert law — Absorbance from transmittance
equations.type: logarithm with change of base, then negation

operator_tree.step2: ln(10)              → cost 1  (10 is a numeric constant terminal, cost 0; ln of it costs 1)

equations.notes: No native log10 primitive in SuperBEST v3. Change-of-base: log10(T)=ln(T)/ln(10). The constant ln(10)≈2.3026 can be pre-folded as a free scalar terminal, reducing to: ln(T)=1 + recip(ln10_const)=2 + mul=2 + neg=2 = 7 nodes. Full tree with explicit ln(10) costs 8.
equations.optimization: If ln(10) is pre-computed as a constant terminal (free), cost reduces to 7 nodes: ln(1)+recip(2)+mul(2)+neg(2).

equations.name: Nernst equation (single ion)

operator_tree.step3: sub(ln([ion]_out), ln([ion]_in))       → cost 2  (= ln([ion]_out/[ion]_in) by log identity)
operator_tree.step4: mul(RT_zF, sub_result)                 → cost 2  (RT/zF is a free composite constant)

equations.notes: The log-ratio identity ln(a/b) = ln(a) − ln(b) replaces a division with a sub, saving the 4-node recip+mul division cost. RT/zF is treated as a single free parameter (temperature, valence, and Faraday's constant absorbed). Total 6 nodes is minimal for a log-ratio expression.
equations.key_insight: Using sub(ln_out, ln_in) instead of ln(recip+mul) saves 2 nodes: sub=2 vs recip(2)+mul(2)=4.

equations.name: Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation (3-ion: K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻)
equations.formula: Vm = (RT/F) · ln((PK[K]o + PNa[Na]o + PCl[Cl]i) / (PK[K]i + PNa[Na]i + PCl[Cl]o))
equations.type: scaled log of a weighted-sum ratio

scaling.step14: mul(RT_F, ln_result)                → cost 2  (RT/F is a free constant)

equations.notes: Each of the three ion terms requires a mul for the permeability-weighted concentration. Each weighted-sum (numerator and denominator) requires 2 add nodes for 3 terms. Division is a recip+mul = 4 nodes. The outer ln costs 1. RT/F is a free parameter.
equations.add_count_explanation: For N terms, N−1 add nodes are needed. With 3 terms: 2 adds per side = 4 adds total, each costing 3 nodes → 12 nodes just for the additions across both sides.
equations.log_ratio_alternative: Could use sub(ln(numer_ratio_terms)…) but the numerator/denominator are sums, not products, so the sub(ln,ln) shortcut from Nernst does NOT apply here. Division is required.

equations.name: One-compartment pharmacokinetics
equations.type: exponential decay — same structure as radioactive decay

operator_tree.step4: mul(C₀, exp_result)  → cost 2  (scale by initial concentration)

equations.notes: Structurally identical to the transmittance form of Beer-Lambert (eq 2). The elimination rate constant k_el plays the same role as εcl. Cost is 7 nodes regardless of whether the exponent is scalar (1 mul) or a product of two variables (2 muls).
equations.alternative: If −k_el is pre-absorbed as a single free parameter k_neg, cost becomes: mul(k_neg,t)=2 + exp=1 + mul(C₀,exp)=2 = 5 nodes.

equations.name: Two-compartment pharmacokinetics
equations.type: bi-exponential — sum of two exponential decay terms
equations.notes: Two independent one-compartment decay subtrees (each 7 nodes) joined by a single add (3 nodes). The variable t is a shared terminal used in both subtrees — free each time (DAG). α and β are the macro-rate constants (combinations of micro-rate constants k12, k21, k10). A and B are pre-fit amplitude constants.
equations.comparison_to_eq6: Two-compartment cost (17) = 2 × one-compartment cost (7) + add (3). The add premium reflects the bi-exponential superposition.

equations.name: Hill equation / Dose-response

shared_power.step1: pow(C, n)                           → cost 3  (shared via DAG in numerator and denominator)

numerator.step4: mul(Emax, Cⁿ)                       → cost 2  (Cⁿ reused from step1)

equations.breakdown: pow(C,n)=3 + pow(EC50,n)=3 + add(3) + mul(2) + recip(2) + mul(2) = 15
equations.dag_note: pow(C,n) appears in both numerator (as Emax·Cⁿ) and denominator (as EC₅₀ⁿ+Cⁿ). With DAG sharing, it is computed once (3 nodes) and referenced twice — no extra cost for the second use.
equations.notes: The two pow nodes (one for C, one for EC₅₀) each cost 3. The denominator add costs 3. Two mul + one recip handle the rational structure. Total 15 nodes with DAG.

equations.name: Fick's first law of diffusion
equations.type: negated product — dC/dx treated as a terminal gradient

operator_tree.step1: mul(D, dC_dx)        → cost 2  (D is diffusion coefficient, dC/dx is concentration gradient terminal)
operator_tree.step2: neg(D · dC_dx)       → cost 2  (applies the sign convention J flows down gradient)

equations.notes: dC/dx (the concentration gradient) is treated as a pre-computed terminal — it is either measured directly or computed in a separate numerical step outside the algebraic expression tree. The law itself is just a scaled negation: J = −D·(dC/dx). Cost matches Beer-Lambert absorbance form (eq 1) plus one neg.
equations.alternative: If the negative sign is absorbed into a signed diffusion coefficient D_neg = −D (free terminal), cost is just mul(D_neg, dC_dx) = 2 nodes.

cross_cutting_findings.cost_range: 4 nodes (Beer-Lambert absorbance, Fick's law) to 31 nodes (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz 3-ion)

structural_archetypes.pure_product: A=εcl and J=−D·dC/dx — 4 nodes each. Simplest possible. Adding neg costs +2.
structural_archetypes.exponential_decay: T=exp(−εcl) and C(t)=C₀exp(−k_el·t) — both 7 nodes. Canonical form: mul+mul+neg+exp.
structural_archetypes.log_ratio_scaled: Nernst — 6 nodes. The sub(ln_a, ln_b) identity avoids a 4-node division, saving 2 nodes over naïve ln(a/b) via recip+mul.
structural_archetypes.rational_with_powers: Hill equation — 15 nodes. Two pow nodes dominate; DAG sharing of Cⁿ is essential.
structural_archetypes.bi_exponential: Two-compartment PK — 17 nodes = 2×7 + 3 (add). Linear superposition of exponentials adds exactly one add(3) per extra term.
structural_archetypes.sum_of_products_ratio: GHK 3-ion — 31 nodes. Dominant cost: 4 mul nodes for permeability-weighted concentrations + 4 add nodes for weighted sums = 6×2+4×3=24 nodes just for numerator+denominator; division+ln+scaling add 7 more.

cross_cutting_findings.log_ratio_identity_benefit: Nernst uses sub(ln_out, ln_in) = 2 nodes instead of ln(recip(in)+mul) = 5 nodes for ln(out/in). This saves 3 nodes. The identity applies only when the ratio argument is itself a ratio of simple terminals, NOT a sum (GHK cannot use this shortcut).
cross_cutting_findings.ghk_vs_nernst_cost_ratio: GHK (31) / Nernst (6) ≈ 5.2×. The explosion comes from the weighted sums in numerator and denominator — each sum of 3 terms costs 12 nodes (3 mul + 2 add), compared to the 2 free terminals in the Nernst log-ratio.
cross_cutting_findings.add_node_as_dominant_cost_driver_in_GHK: Each add costs 3 nodes — the most expensive primitive per operation. GHK has 4 add nodes (2 per side) = 12 nodes from adds alone. Reducing ion count to 2 ions would eliminate 2 adds (−6 nodes) and 2 muls (−4 nodes), reducing GHK to 21 nodes.
cross_cutting_findings.neg_vs_sub: neg(x) costs 2 nodes; sub(a,b) costs 2 nodes. For a single negation use neg; for a difference use sub. Both are equal cost.
cross_cutting_findings.pow_cost: pow(x,n) at 3 nodes is the most expensive single-argument operator — more expensive than exp(1) or ln(1). The Hill equation pays 6 nodes just for the two power terms.
cross_cutting_findings.exp_is_cheapest_transcendental: exp costs only 1 node — cheaper than neg(2), recip(2), or pow(3). The expensive part of exponential-decay expressions is building the exponent (mul+neg), not the exp itself.
cross_cutting_findings.compartment_model_scaling: An N-compartment model with N exponential terms costs 7N + 3(N−1) = 10N−3 nodes: N decay subtrees plus N−1 add nodes chaining them.
primitives_never_used_in_this_block: pow used only in Hill; no equation uses both pow and exp simultaneously
